chain_of_thought: Native crash in strchr_default suggests a C library strchr being called with invalid pointer or non-null-terminated data.
Top app-native frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, called by Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse. Tools are unavailable, so I must reason from naming and typical TBXML usage.
TBXML is a small XML parser that typically takes a C string buffer and passes it to various string functions, including strchr, while parsing tags and attributes.
JNI method jniParse([B)J suggests Java passes a raw byte[] to native; decodeBytes likely converts this byte array into a char* or similar and feeds it into the XML parser.

If decodeBytes fails to enforce that the input buffer is null-terminated or to limit length, then calls to strchr on that buffer can read past the end of the allocated region, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads and crashes.

Because the fuzzer harness calls jniParse([B)J directly with arbitrary bytes, this execution path is reachable with attacker-controlled input. In the normal app, jniParse is ultimately driven by loadKml(String) and parse(String), which in many apps are fed from network or file KML data, plausibly attacker-controlled (e.g., remote map overlays).

The specific termination in strchr_default aligns with an out-of-bounds read on a non-terminated or corrupted string rather than a deliberate abort; no evidence suggests the harness passed an impossible value like a null JNIEnv or invalid jbyteArray, just arbitrary data in the byte buffer.

Even without decompilation, the function names and stack strongly indicate unchecked use of C string APIs on attacker-controlled buffers; there is no hint of length validation or safe wrappers between jniParse and decodeBytes.

This represents at least a memory safety bug (CWE-787/CWE-120) and a realistic denial-of-service in production, and possibly stronger impact depending on allocator and surrounding code, though with current evidence DoS is clear but RCE is speculative.

Tool connections failed, so I can’t provide exact code snippets or addresses; severity is assessed as high due to clear memory-corruption potential from unsafe string handling on untrusted input, but confidence slightly below 0.9 due to lack of direct source view.
reasons: Crash occurs in libc strchr_default after decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, indicating unsafe C-string handling leading to out-of-bounds access
jniParse([B)J passes attacker-controlled byte[] data from Java into native code with no visible length or termination checks before decodeBytes uses strchr
Call path from parse/loadKml suggests realistic use with externally supplied KML/XML data, so malformed input can reliably trigger the crash as a DoS and likely memory corruption

evidence.note: Top crashing frame; indicates C library strchr called on invalid or unterminated string buffer
First app-native frame beneath libc; likely converts incoming bytes to C string and uses strchr without enforcing null-termination or bounds
JNI entry taking byte[] and forwarding to decodeBytes; no indication of bounds checking between Java and native

recommendations: Review and decompile decodeBytes and related TBXML parsing functions to confirm how the byte[] is converted and ensure all uses of strchr/strcpy/strlen are bounded by the actual buffer length
Enforce explicit buffer length handling: either append a safe null terminator within allocated space or use length-aware APIs (memchr, strn* variants) with robust bounds checks
Add robust fuzz and negative testing around jniParse([B)J using libFuzzer/AFL to surface additional malformed-input crashes and validate fixes
assumptions: decodeBytes uses strchr (or similar) on the byte buffer passed by jniParse without adequate length or termination checks
The byte[] passed to jniParse can be influenced by untrusted external KML/XML data in realistic app usage

No direct view of Java implementation of TBXML.jniParse([B)J or TBXML.parse(String), so input validation on the Java side cannot be fully ruled out

chain_of_thought: Tools are unreachable, so I must reason only from the structured crash info.
The top native frame is strpbrk, a standard C library string-scanning function that requires a NUL-terminated char*; crashes typically occur if given a non-terminated or invalid pointer.
The first app-level function below that is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, called from the JNI entry Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse.

Because I cannot decompile, I infer typical TBXML behavior: parse XML from a byte buffer, often passed from Java as a byte[] for an XML string; decodeBytes likely converts this buffer into a C string and then calls strpbrk to scan for delimiters or XML tokens.

If decodeBytes directly passes a pointer from JNI (e.g., GetByteArrayElements) and a length, but uses strpbrk without ensuring that the buffer is NUL-terminated within bounds, strpbrk can read past the buffer, potentially causing memory corruption or crash. That is a classic out-of-bounds read condition.

The fuzz harness passes arbitrary bytes in fuzz_one_input, so the byte[] may contain no zero-byte and may not be designed as a C string. In real app use, the Java side likely passes valid UTF-8/ASCII XML strings, which always have a terminating 0 at the JNI boundary when converted by the VM (if they are passed as jstring), but here we see the JNI entry takes a byte[] directly, not a jstring.

Because the JNI function explicitly uses a byte array [B, the native code must itself ensure correct termination; it cannot rely on the JVM automatically creating a NUL terminator. Therefore, if decodeBytes assumes a C string but fails to create/ensure a terminator based on the array length, that is a genuine unsafe design, not just a fuzz-only problem.

However, I cannot inspect the implementation to confirm whether they allocate a new buffer with length+1, copy bytes, set buf[len]=0, and then pass that to strpbrk. If they do, it is safe. If they directly use the raw pointer, it is unsafe.

I also cannot see any bounds or length checks; no evidence of validation appears in the stack. Still, the crash reason explicitly naming strpbrk suggests an invalid read triggered by the fuzzer, consistent with missing termination or invalid pointer.

The Java call graph shows TBXML.jniParse([B)J being called from parse(String) → PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) etc.; in normal flows, the String is likely converted to bytes (e.g., getBytes()), and the code may assume it is text/XML. Yet an attacker controlling KML input (file/network) can cause arbitrary byte sequences to reach the native layer, so this path is realistically attacker-influenced.

Given the absence of evidence of validation and the common anti-pattern of using C string APIs over raw byte[] without explicit NUL-termination, I treat this as a likely out-of-bounds read / memory safety issue, but not fully confirmed (no code).

Impact-wise, this is at least a DoS from malformed input, and potentially more severe (OOB read, info leak) but RCE is less certain. Classify as memory safety vulnerability (CWE-125 or CWE-787) with medium-to-high severity and moderate confidence (~0.7).
reasons: Crash occurs inside strpbrk called from app function decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, suggesting misuse of C string API on untrusted data.
JNI entry TBXML.jniParse([B)J receives a raw byte[] from Java, so native code must ensure proper NUL-termination before calling strpbrk; lack of such checks can cause out-of-bounds read.
Fuzzer-driven arbitrary byte[] input and realistic attacker-controlled KML/XML data via PluginKmlOverlay imply this path can be influenced by external input, leading to a potential memory-safety DoS.

evidence.snippet: Standard C string scanning function that assumes a NUL-terminated string; crash indicates likely invalid or unterminated buffer.
evidence.note: Top native frame where process terminates.
Direct caller of strpbrk; name suggests conversion/decoding of raw bytes into text before scanning, likely using C string APIs.
JNI bridge receiving Java byte[] ([B) and passing its data pointer into decodeBytes without visible validation in stack trace.
JNI bridge method signature confirms raw byte[] is passed, requiring explicit length-based handling and termination in native code.

recommendations: Review decodeBytes and surrounding code to ensure any buffer passed to strpbrk is allocated with length+1 and explicitly NUL-terminated based on the byte[] length.
Replace C string APIs like strpbrk with length-aware functions that take an explicit buffer length, or add rigorous bounds checks before scanning.
Add fuzz tests and negative unit tests on TBXML.jniParse([B)J with random and malformed byte[] input to reproduce and validate the fix.
assumptions: Native implementation of decodeBytes uses strpbrk directly on data derived from the Java byte[] without always ensuring a terminating NUL inside the allocated range.
The crash reason "strpbrk" corresponds to an invalid memory access (e.g., reading past buffer) rather than an unrelated abort.
Attacker-controlled KML/XML or other inputs can influence the content of the byte[] passed into TBXML.jniParse([B)J in production flows.
limitations: Unable to decompile libtbxml.so to inspect decodeBytes or JNI implementation details.
No direct visibility into Java-side argument preparation for TBXML.jniParse([B)J (e.g., how Strings are converted to byte[] or any additional validation).
Exact faulting address and memory layout at crash time are not available, so I cannot conclusively prove out-of-bounds versus other failure modes.
